DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-07-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. REID ERLICH, Esq. of Shernoff Bidart Echeverria LLP (tel: 909-621-4935) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. MARY MCPHERSON, Esq. of Tressler LLP (tel: 949-336-1200) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CSE SAFEGUARD INSURANCE COMPANY's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' demurrers to plaintiffs' complaint are overruled.

Peter, Paul, Diane and Karen DeMartini owned a row house in San Francisco's Sunset district. On February 9, 2023, a tenant allegedly making hash oil triggered an explosion that destroyed the house and killed his wife. On January 5, 2024, the DeMartinis sued defendant insurers.
The 44-page complaint adequately pleads breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing - in 17 ways. (Id. at 34:1-37:25.) Defendants focus narrowly on nonpayment of benefits (Memo. 7:14-27, 9:11-16), but insurance bad faith occurs in a host of ways. (CACI 2300-2361; see also Schwartz v. State Farm Fire & Casualty Co. (2001) 88 Cal.App.4th 1329, 1339.) Further, the complaint adequately alleges a joint venture among the three CSE entities, which call themselves the "CSE Insurance Group" and are all located in the same Los Angeles suburb. (Cmplt. 7:14-24, 6:20-7:5; Memo. 8:3-9:10.) Defendants end by fighting the pled facts (id. at 9:17-24), while elsewhere conceding they may not do so. (Id. at 9:5-6; Del E. Webb Corp. v. Structural Materials Corp. (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d 593, 604.)

The complaint adequately pleads breach of contract. (Id. at 38:1-39:19.) Defendants demand "evidence" (Memo. 10:21), but only allegations are needed at the pleading stage. Nor is particularized pleading required, as for certain fraud claims. (Id. at 10:16-19.) Defendants argue that no right to independent counsel arose. (Id. at 11:4-5.) Even were that so, it would not matter, for the complaint pleads other breaches and only one suffices. (See Elder v. Pacific Bell Telephone Co. (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 841, 856 n.14.)

The complaint adequately pleads a UCL claim. (Id. at 40:1-42:26.) Defendants repeat arguments already addressed above. Moreover, alternative claims may be pled - e.g., contract breach and UCL. (See Memo. 12:18-25; Adams v. Paul (1995) 11 Cal.4th 583, 593.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
